Julie Tetel Andresen's initial academic career focused on linguistic historiography, particularly French, German, and American theories of language from the eighteenth through the twentieth centuries. Her research interests evolved to encompass a developmental approach to linguistics and evolution. Andresen investigates how history, culture, and politics shape language. She is known for her critical historical approach and has contributed to linguistic theory through her work in linguistic historiography.

Rice attended Rutgers University–New Brunswick, where he played football for coach Greg Schiano's Scarlet Knights from 2005 to 2007. In Rice's freshman year, Rutgers had its first winning season in 13 years and played in only the second bowl in the football program's then 136-year history. In 2006, the Scarlet Knights won a school record-tying 11 games and registered the school's highest-ever season-ending national poll ranking, finishing at #12 in both the Associated Press and Coaches Polls.

In 1946, Lombardi was placed in auxiliary; he would be promoted to full admiral in 1952. He then moved to Argentina, where he served as consul of Italy in Tucuman; he returned to Italy in 1957. He died in Rome in 1978.

Bobby Halpern, born in 1931 (though Boxrec lists his birth date as January 1, 1936), was raised in the Little Italy section of the Bronx, New York. The son of a Jewish father and an Irish mother, Halpern grew up in a mixed ethnic neighborhood where fighters were revered. He became involved in street fighting and, after discovering his talent for it, began training as an amateur boxer at the age of 15. In 1948, he won the 118-pound New Jersey Diamond Gloves championship and a New Jersey state title. Over the next five years, Halpern had close to 200 amateur bouts, losing approximately 10. However, his burgeoning boxing career was sidetracked by legal troubles that led to a prison sentence.

Cormac McCarthy's novel *The Road* garnered significant recognition. In 2006, McCarthy was awarded the James Tait Black Memorial Prize in fiction and the Believer Book Award, and was a finalist for the National Book Critics Circle Award. On April 16, 2007, *The Road* won the Pulitzer Prize for fiction, and in 2012, it was shortlisted for the Best of the James Tait Black.

In June 2012, Mood Media acquired BIS Group, a company specializing in audio-visual installations for corporate and commercial clients. Operating in Belgium, Luxembourg and the Netherlands, this acquisition extends Mood Media’s reach into Europe. The deal consisted of a 22.5 million Euro cash buyout.

Phil Masi is best remembered for his involvement in a controversial play during Game 1 of the 1948 World Series, held at Braves Field. The game pitted the Boston Braves against the Cleveland Indians and featured a scoreless pitching duel between Cleveland's Bob Feller and Boston's Johnny Sain. In the bottom of the eighth inning, Feller walked Braves catcher Bill Salkeld, who was then replaced by Masi as a pinch runner. Mike McCormick then executed a sacrifice bunt, advancing Masi to second base. Feller attempted to pick Masi off second, but umpire Bill Stewart called Masi safe, a decision that was disputed by Feller and the Indians. Tommy Holmes then singled to left field, scoring Masi with the only run of the game, giving the Braves a 1-0 victory. Despite the controversial call, Cleveland went on to win the 1948 World Series four games to two.

*The Joy Luck Club* garnered widespread critical acclaim upon its release. The film holds a 90% "Fresh" rating on Rotten Tomatoes. The site's consensus praises the film's emotional depth and strong performances. Audiences polled by CinemaScore gave the film an "A" grade. Both Gene Siskel and Roger Ebert included *The Joy Luck Club* on their top ten films of 1993, alongside *Schindler's List*, *The Piano*, and *The Age of Innocence*. Siskel, who ranked the film at number 7 on his list, lauded the film's script and performances, highlighting its ability to connect with audiences on an emotional level. Ebert placed the film at number 8 on his list.

After filming for four days in 2013, the films duration was too long to be a short film but too short to be a full feature. The short film soon turned into a feature film with a plethora of Scottish cast coming on board the production. The filming occurred within 2 weeks over a one-year period. Production took place in under three weeks over 2013 and 2014. Sponsor contributions made up £5,000 production budget. The release date was put back as Dewar and Grieve appealed for assistance to get the project finished.

Danon Scorpion began his three-year-old campaign on February 13 at Tokyo Racecourse in the G3 Kyodo News Hai, also known as the Tokinominoru Kinen, an 1,800-meter race. He finished seventh in the race. Next, he was entered in the G3 Arlington Cup over 1,600 meters at Hanshin Racecourse on April 16, where he started as the favorite. In a field of 18 runners, Danon Scorpion, ridden by Yuga Kawada, sprinted at a steady pace and sat in the middle of the pack early on before gaining position as the race progressed. He overtook Matenro Orion to win by a neck.

Cliff Lubwa P’chong was born in Gulu, Uganda. He was educated at Koc Goma Primary School, then Gulu High School for his junior secondary school before joing Sir Samuel Baker School, Gulu, for his O'levels, then National Teachers' College Kyambogo where he qualified as a grade five teacher in 1969, before being posted to St Charles Lwanga SSS, Koboko. In 1976 he attained a bachelor's degree in literature and linguistics from the prestigious Makerere University, before proceeding to Durham and Exeter universities. He was a creative writing fellow at the University of Iowa (1987), and lectured in drama-in-education at the Institute of Teacher Education, Kyambogo. He had his early education there and in Kyambogo. He taught for several years, and then studied literature and linguistics at Makerere University.

Nati Kaji began his musical journey at the age of seven, playing the harmonium at the Gujeshwori Bhajan Mandali. His professional career started in 1950 when he joined Radio Nepal, where he served for 40 years. During this time, he composed over 2000 songs across various genres and is credited with over 15 operas, including *Pijada ko Suga*. He worked at Radio Nepal as a Nayab Subba from 1951 to 1978. One of his well-known compositions is "Marna Baru Garho Hunna," with lyrics written by Tirtharaj Tuladhar.
Buskohl needed emergency surgery on his right arm after being attacked. Surgery saved his arm. However, it was unclear if his ring finger would be saved.

Some users reported unexpected battery shutdowns after updating to iOS 10.1.1, with devices sometimes dying with 30 to 50% charge remaining. One user described an unusual behavior where the battery percentage would decrease significantly after the device was shut down. Apple began addressing this issue with iOS 10.2.1, stating that it had made improvements to reduce the occurrence of unexpected shutdowns. Following the release of iOS 10.2.1, Apple claimed to have seen an 80% reduction in unexpected shutdowns on iPhone 6s models and a 70% reduction on iPhone 6 models. After the update, the phone could restart without needing to connect to power.

Located in Hanover, Germany, the Aegidienkirche, or Aegidia Church, stands as a war memorial. The present Gothic building, constructed in 1347 on the site of a 12th-century Romanesque church, was never rebuilt after sustaining damage in World War II. The church was first mentioned in 1163, and a chapel is thought to have existed on the site as early as the 10th century. The sandstone used in the church's reconstruction came from the Deister region. The original church was dedicated to Saint Giles, a Greek Abbot who lived as a hermit in France.

By 1939, the New Zealand Government was becoming increasingly concerned about the likelihood of war in Europe. It was therefore decided to expand the New Zealand Military Forces and in May 1939 a major reorganisation was undertaken. The territorial forces were expanded and a new reserve force called the National Military Reserve was formed. The National Military Reserve was to act as a pool of manpower for the Territorial Force in the event of war and also provide a force of less able men for menial work. Recruits were designated into three classes:

The Wheeler Opera House's first story has historically housed businesses, including a bank and barber shop. The second story contains the theater lobby. The lobby features plaster, and a mural depicting Balboa's 16th-century sailing ship. Renovations have reconfigured the first and second-floor lobbies. A fire stair tower with a freight elevator and a passenger elevator were added to improve accessibility. A grand staircase leads from the entrance to the auditorium. A 19th-century safe purchased by Jerome Wheeler is on display in the revamped lobby.
Nuria Calderon Garcia-Botey, known professionally as Nuria C. Botey, is a Spanish fantasy, science fiction, and horror writer. Context 1 indicates that she is a writer of science fiction.

The time from building to entering operational service included sea trials to test the ship's systems and to train personnel. Only then did carriers move to intended war zones. Construction of HMS Victorious began in 1937 but she did not become combat operational until mid-1941 when rushed into the hunt for the German battleship Bismarck with only a quarter of her aircraft onboard . The USS Essex began in April 1941 and she was operational in mid-1943, in a raid on Rabaul eight months after commissioning.

Dawoud participated in the 2011–12 Saudi Arabian protests during the Arab Spring. Originally, in March 2012, he was questioned by Saudi police and asked to be an informant and report details about his fellow protesters. After he refused, Saudi security forces arrested him from the Dammam Central Hospital, where he was undergoing treatment for an eye injury sustained in a traffic accident. Saudi forces surrounded the hospital and arrested him as he prepared for surgery.

Area15, located just off Interstate 15 near Desert Inn Road in Las Vegas, is housed in a massive black structure. Its minimal exterior appearance has been noted by co-creator Michael Beneville, who stated that the challenge was to create something additive to Las Vegas' already imaginative landscape. One side of the building features light building elements. The building encompasses 200,000 square feet. Upon arrival, visitors are greeted by Art Island, an outdoor gallery featuring large-scale sculptures that act as a "palate cleanser from the ordinary world." The parking lot also displays a defunct airplane and expansive murals.

The Australian colony of Victoria received six 35 cwt guns in August 1865. They were used as mobile coast fortification guns with one gun being fitted to the colonial sloop Victoria during 1866 & 1867. Later four of the guns were used as field guns at Hastings. Three of these guns are known to survive.
The play was filmed for Australian TV in 1965 by the Australian Broadcasting Corporation. In 1961, the ABC produced *A Little South of Heaven*.

A 'Memorandum for distribution' issued by the US Navy defined two annexed reports as factual accounts intended to promote transparency. In the interest of transparency, the memorandum authorized the release of these reports. The reports specifically excluded an examination of the actions of the other vessel involved. The report for John S. McCain detailed the actions of the ship and its crew, but not those of Alnic MC.

Having been stripped of his administrative office by the new Brătianu cabinet, Macedonski faced financial difficulties, and was forced to move into a house on the outskirts of Bucharest, and later moved between houses in northern Bucharest. According to Călinescu, the poet continued to cultivate luxury and passionately invested in the decorative arts, although his source of income, other than the supposed assistance "of [European] ruling houses", remains a mystery. Arguing that Macedonski was "always in need of money" to use on his luxury items, poet Victor Eftimiu claimed: "He did not shy away from sending emphatic notes to the potentates of his day [...], flattering some, threatening others. He would marry off or simply mate some of his disciples with aging and rich women, and then he would squeeze out their assets."

The Brazil men's national goalball team competed at the 2019 Parapan American Games, held in Lima, Peru, from August 23 to September 1. The tournament was a qualifier for the Tokyo 2020 Paralympic Games. Eight nations participated in the men's goalball event, including the USA, Peru, and Venezuela.

Katakwi is a town in the Eastern Region of Uganda. It is located approximately 52 kilometers (32 mi) northeast of Soroti, the largest city in the Teso sub-region. Katakwi lies about 153 kilometers (95 mi) northwest of Mbale, the largest city in Uganda's Eastern Region. Kampala, the capital of Uganda, is approximately 343 kilometers (213 mi) southwest of Katakwi. The coordinates of the town are 1°54′54.0″N 33°57′18.0″E.

Speculations and imaginings about Purgatory are diverse, reflecting conflicting ideas among Catholic saints and theologians regarding its nature. The popular image often includes notions of a concrete, visitable place of fiery purification, though recent Catholic thought, as noted by Paul J. Griffiths, preserves the essentials of the basic doctrine while offering a more modern approach. This approach moves away from the idea of Purgatory as a place of punishment. Then-Cardinal Joseph Ratzinger (later Pope Benedict XVI) famously wrote that Purgatory is not a "supra-worldly concentration camp," but rather an "inwardly necessary process of transformation" that makes a person capable of Christ, God, and unity with the communion of saints.

DecembeRadio, the self-titled debut album by the Dove Award-winning band of the same name, was released in 2006. The album followed Michael Jackson's *Dangerous*, which contained songs that addressed race, poverty, and social issues with a modern twist. In contrast, much of *DecembeRadio* is rooted in hard rock reminiscent of bands like King's X, Atomic Opera, and Aerosmith, with tracks like "Razor" and "Table" showcasing this powerful edge. The opening riff of "Live and Breathe" exemplifies the band's technical skill. Front-man Sam Bentley's folk influences are evident in the jangly guitar sound, recalling 1980s pop, achieved through finger-picking on a clean-sounding electric guitar. The album's first single, "Drifter," earned the band a Dove Award nomination for Song of the Year. Acoustic guitar features prominently on "Love and War," a powerful song with a Spanish-style guitar, and is also present on "Waiting on a Miracle." Scotty Wilbanks, known for his work with adult contemporary acts, produced the album, letting his "inner rock child run wild" and adding B3 organ to the band's sound.

The first race of the season was the Argentine Grand Prix, where Hill qualified 17th, although Edwards was on the back row of the grid. In the race, Hill was running tenth when his engine failed with a few laps remaining while Edwards finished 11th, two laps down on the winner Denny Hulme. In Brazil, Edwards qualified 25th and last, over two seconds slower than his nearest rival Richard Robarts, and Hill qualified 21st. The race was rain shortened and Hill finished 11th after Edwards retired on lap three with chassis problems. At the South African Grand Prix, Embassy Hill entered only one car, for Hill, who qualified 18th and finished 12th.

Hart was born in Tampa, Florida, and graduated from Winter Park High School. He attended Seminole Junior College where he was catcher on the baseball team. In 1969 he won All-American honors and began his professional career as a catcher in the Montreal Expos organization. He caught with them for three seasons before leaving the organization and returning to Florida. He graduated in 1973 from the University of Central Florida with majors in history and physical education.

Added in patch 2.3, Kanai's Cube is an improvement on the Horadric Cubes used in Diablo II. In Adventure Mode, players will speak to the ghost of Zoltun Kulle in town, at which point he will direct them to the Ruins of Sescheron in Act III to locate the cube. Once found, the cube is available to all characters of the type that finds it (normal or Hardcore; the first character in a new Season must re-acquire it regardless of whether their normal or Hardcore characters have already found it). With certain crafting material combinations, the cube can be used to extract legendary powers (marked in orange text in the item's stats), upgrade rare items to legendary, remove level requirements on items, and other abilities.

In a season limited due to the ongoing COVID-19 pandemic, the Raiders finished the 2020–21 season 18–6, 16–4 in Horizon League play to finish as Horizon League regular season co-champions, alongside Cleveland State. However, as the No. 2 seed in the Horizon League tournament, they were upset by Milwaukee in the quarterfinals.

Pringle had reached the rank of Major in 1900 when he was appointed an inspecting officer in the Board of Trade. At the start of World War I in 1914, he was appointed a deputy director of railway transport with the temporary rank of Colonel. In 1916 he was confirmed in this rank when he returned to the Board of Trade as Chief Inspecting Officer. When the Ministry of Transport was formed in 1919, he was transferred to the new ministry with his department, retaining his position as Chief Inspecting Officer until his retirement in 1929.

In January 1945, the Casemate d'Esch saw extensive action and sustained damage during the German Operation Nordwind offensive, which began on January 9th. The fighting in the Hatten area involved the US VI Corps and the German XXXIX Panzer Corps, including elements of the 21st Panzer Division and the 25th Panzergrenadier Division. The 14th Armored Division, along with elements of the 79th Infantry Division, were involved in the fighting around Hatten. The fighting continued until January 19th, when the Americans pulled back. The 14th Armored Division suffered heavy casualties, losing all of its tanks. 83 civilians were killed in Hatten. On 18 March 1945, the area was recaptured.

On the review aggregator website Rotten Tomatoes, Daniel Isn't Real holds an approval rating of 84% based on 82 reviews, with an average of 7.2/10. The critical consensus reads "Daniel Isn't Real, but the smart, stylish fun waiting for genre lovers in this well-acted suspense thriller is completely genuine." On Metacritic, the film holds an averaged score of 61 out of 100 based on 11 critic reviews, indicating "generally favorable reviews".

Paul Merton's breakthrough as a television performer came in 1988 with Channel 4's improvised comedy show *Whose Line Is It Anyway?*, which had moved to television from BBC Radio 4. Prior to this, he had performed on Channel 4's *Saturday Live* and compered its series *Comedy Wavelength* in 1987. Merton remained on *Whose Line* until 1993. In 1990, he began appearing on the satirical news quiz *Have I Got News for You*, and two series of his own sketch show, *Paul Merton: The Drawings*, followed soon after. In 1994, Merton presented a two-part documentary series about the London Palladium, titled *Paul Merton's Palladium Story*. In 1996, Merton performed updated versions of fifteen scripts by Ray Galton and Alan Simpson for an ITV series, *Paul Merton in Galton & Simpson's*. The updated scripts met with mixed reviews, and the complete series was released on DVD in June 2007.

On the edition of January 10 of TNA Impact! Anderson made her Total Nonstop Action Wrestling (TNA) debut as an unknown woman wearing a niqāb, later named Raisha Saeed and billed from Syria. She was in Awesome Kong's corner when Kong defeated Gail Kim to win the TNA Women's Knockout Championship. Her first pay-per-view appearance with the company was again in Kong's corner at Against All Odds, distracting Kong's opponent ODB to help her retain the Knockout Championship. The situation with Kim and ODB intensified, leading to Anderson's first match as a competitor with the promotion. At TNA's all-steel cage event Lockdown, she and Kong took on Kim and ODB in a tag team match, where Saeed was pinned by ODB. On the edition of July 3, 2008 of Impact!, Saeed lost a match against newcomer Taylor Wilde which saw Wilde become number one contender for the Knockout Championship despite outside interference from Kong.

In November 2013, Il Divo released their sixth studio album, *A Musical Affair*, featuring Broadway tunes arranged for a quartet. To promote the album, Il Divo performed *Il Divo - A Musical Affair: The Greatest Songs of Broadway Live* at the Marquis Theatre in New York, beginning November 7th. *Amor & Pasion* is Il Divo's seventh studio album.

TSLS provides two schemes; Scholarship and Loan. Under the Scholarship scheme, TSLS provides local and overseas scholarships as well as for students with special needs. The Loan scheme are funded in the form of loans that are not normal debts. Since its establishment, the Government has paid out $1.2 billion for the schemes.

Lectionary 228 (Gregory-Aland numbering) is a Greek manuscript containing lessons from the Gospels, Acts, and Epistles, as well as lessons from the Old Testament lectionary. The text is written in minuscule letters. The New Testament section of the codex consists of 227 paper leaves, each measuring 29.5 cm by 22.8 cm. The text is arranged in two columns per page, with 22 lines per page. The entire codex comprises a total of 391 leaves.

The 1998 Football League Second Division play-off final took place on 24 May 1998 at Wembley Stadium, with a kick-off time of 3:00 pm BST. Grimsby Town faced Northampton Town in a match that saw Grimsby secure promotion to Division 1 at the first time of asking after their relegation the previous season. A crowd of almost 70,000, though reported elsewhere as 63,733, witnessed Kevin Donovan score the only goal of the match, securing a 1-0 victory for the Mariners. Donovan's goal, described by one commentator as "the only highlight of a dreadfully dull first half," came after a well-placed pass. Northampton goalkeeper Woodman was unable to prevent Donovan's decisive strike. Grimsby could have doubled their lead three minutes later when Jack Lester had a shot with only Woodman to beat, but his effort went wide.

The 2015 Chongming Island World Cup, a 125-kilometre race, started on roads that led to the Shanghai Yangtze River. The course, known for its flat landscape and sprint-friendly nature, featured few changes in altitude and typical road conditions for the area. Riders navigated corners on the course while battling crosswinds that often split the peloton. The route included a 10-km underwater tunnel to Changxing Island, followed by the 8-km Shanghai Yangtze River Bridge to Chongming Island.

George H. Coes went to California in 1852 and was associated with minstrel acts in San Francisco. He opened with Woods and Christy's Minstrels in New York City in 1862. Coes returned to California in 1865 to form Coes and Wells' Minstrels with Sam Wells. The partnership dissolved after a short time. On March 19, 1867, Coes joined with S.S. Purdy and Frank Converse to form a new minstrel company, Coes, Purdy and Converse's Party, which opened in Troy, New York.

Mulan's romantic relationship with Li Shang, while a key element of the animated film, has faced scrutiny from critics. Some argue that the character of Li Shang relies on "stereotypical, misogynistic, and rigid masculinity," making him unsuitable for the film's progressive themes. Critics have also accused the film of reinforcing "heteronormative tropes" in its ending, where Mulan finds an "acceptable potential husband." Despite these criticisms, Roger Ebert, film critic for the *Chicago Sun-Times*, observed that the film "manages somehow to be true simultaneously to feminist dogma and romantic convention." Ebert noted that the film breaks the tradition of Western children's classics, being based on a Chinese folk tale. However, he also stated that the movie is still sold on the idea that Mulan finds herself an acceptable potential husband. Janet Maslin of *The New York Times* negatively opined about Mulan's need for "Mr. Right."

The Lottery also offers Print 'n Play games. As with traditional Lottery games, tickets are printed by the terminal; however as in scratchers, winning status are determined when the ticket is printed (there is no drawing). Originally called Fast Play with a continually-changing lineup of games at $2, $3, and $5 prices, each with its own rules and prizes, when it became Print 'n Play the lineup was simplified to the three most popular games—Bingo, Blackjack, and Crossword—and a new $10 tier was added for Bingo and Crossword. A $20 tier has recently been added along with a new game: Print 'n Play × (Times) the Money, based on a popular series of scratchers.

Following Edward "Butch" O'Hare's death, Chicago Tribune owner Robert McCormick proposed renaming Orchard Depot Airport in his honor. On September 19, 1949, the airport was officially renamed O'Hare International Airport as a tribute to the WWII hero. An F4F Wildcat, painted like O'Hare's aircraft, is displayed in Terminal 2.

Born in Seville, he was the son of Brigadier José de Gayangos, intendente of Zacatecas, in New Spain (Mexico). After completing his primary education in Madrid, at the age of thirteen he was sent to school at Pont-le-Voy near Blois. Then, he began the study of Arabic in the École spéciale des Langues orientales of Paris under Silvestre de Sacy.

Coe was made an Honorary Doctor of Technology (Hon DTech) by his alma mater, Loughborough University in 1985. In November 2009, he was awarded an honorary degree as Doctor of Science (Hon DSc) from the University of East London. In 2009, he also was awarded an Honorary Fellow of the Royal Institute of British Architects. He also received an honorary Doctorate of Letters from the University of Sunderland in 2011.

Bruni attended The Loomis Chaffee School, a co-educational boarding school located in Windsor, Connecticut. He then pursued his undergraduate studies at the University of North Carolina at Chapel Hill, graduating in 1986 with a Bachelor of Arts degree and Phi Beta Kappa honors. As a Morehead Scholar, he majored in English and wrote for *The Daily Tar Heel*. Subsequently, Bruni earned a Master of Science degree in journalism from Columbia University Graduate School of Journalism in 1988, graduating second in his class and winning a Pulitzer Traveling Fellowship.

On August 28, 2017, Lis Shoshi signed with Sigal Prishtina. He later parted ways with the team and joined Pieno žvaigždės for the 2017–18 season. On November 11, 2017, Shoshi recorded a season-high 25 efficiency for Kosovo. On April 21, 2018, Shoshi scored a season-high 20 points and grabbed 6 rebounds in a loss against Lietkabelis. Shoshi won the King Mindaugas Cup with Pieno žvaigždės in 2018.

Ambivalent sexism reflects the duality of hostility towards women and the tendency for women to be rated more positively than men in surveys. Hostile sexism impacts those perceived as nontraditional women who threaten male power, for example, female professionals and intellectuals, feminists, and political lesbians. Conversely, benevolent sexism protects women who are perceived as adhering to traditional gender roles, such as housewives and secretaries. Fiske asserts that these two forms of sexism comprise ambivalence. On the one hand, women are viewed as competent but not warm, while on the other hand, they are viewed as warm but incompetent. In the workplace, nontraditional women tend to suffer from hostile sexism since they are viewed as competitors. As benevolent sexism includes perceived obligations of protection and help, it leads to women being viewed as less worthy of hiring, training, and promoting due to the concern of them being less able to effectively manage both personal and professional. Fiske contends that when addressing bias against women, both demeaning benevolence and dangerous hostility must be account for.

Once babies are born they are placed on a delivery mat until the mother delivers the placenta. The umbilical cord is then cut with a razor blade or sugar cane peel. Afterwards both the mother and the baby are bathed. Postpartum visits usually take place within the first 6 weeks after the birth of the child.

Levine announced his candidacy for Manhattan borough president in January 2020. Levine was endorsed by Representatives Adriano Espaillat and Nydia Velázquez, among other elected officials, labor unions, and advocacy groups. He won the Democratic primary defeating New York State Senator Brad Hoylman by 7%, and defeated Republican Lou Puliafito in the November 2021 general election.

After graduating from high school, Carl Brumbaugh attended the University of Florida, where he played for the Florida Gators football team. He was a halfback for the Gators from 1926 to 1928, alternating with Red Bethea. In 1928, Brumbaugh was part of the legendary "Phantom Four" backfield, along with Clyde Crabtree, Royce Goodbread, and Rainey Cawthon, which led the nation in scoring. The 1928 Gators, coached by Charles Bachman, scored 376 points. The team finished the season with an 8-1 record, their only loss a narrow 13-12 defeat to Tennessee. Brumbaugh was later inducted into the University of Florida Athletic Hall of Fame as a "Gator Great."

Five-term Republican incumbent and Senate Minority Leader Mitch McConnell had been re-elected with 53% of the vote in 2008. McConnell sought re-election to a sixth term. McConnell defeated businessman Matt Bevin in the Republican primary on May 20.

Prior to 2007, PA 830 ran further west into Washington and Pine Creek Townships. The route began at US 322 near Emerickville. It had an interchange with I-80 at Exit 86 and traveled a winding route heading northeast. At Red Barn Road, it made a right turn and passed the old entrance to the DuBois Regional Airport. It made a sharp right curve at Allens Mills Road where the present routing continues today. The route between US 322 and Red Barn Road is still state-maintained as SR 1830 and the section of Red Barn Road between SR 1830 and the modern-day PA 830 is SR 1013. Parts of the old PA 830 are still drivable between the PA 830 / SR 1830 intersection and Allens Mills Road, but it is not completely passable due to breaks in the pavement and the new parking lot for the airport.

Energy production boomed during the Obama administration. An increase in oil production was driven largely by a fracking boom spurred by private investment on private land, and played only a small role in this development. The Obama administration promoted the growth of renewable energy, and solar power generation tripled during Obama's presidency. Obama also issued numerous energy efficiency standards, contributing to a flattening of growth of the total U.S. energy demand. In May 2010, Obama extended a moratorium on offshore drilling permits after the 2010 Deepwater Horizon oil spill, which is generally considered to be the worst oil spill in U.S. history. In December 2016, President Obama invoked the Outer Continental Shelf Lands Act to ban offshore oil and gas exploration in large parts of the Arctic and Atlantic Oceans.

'Dasein' depicts a trekker's journey of self-discovery, mirroring the philosophical concept of "being there" (Dasein). The film follows Aniket Chatterjee, a young painter, as he approaches death by suicide. As he dies, Aniket realizes the fragility of life and the importance of meaning. Occasionally, the film's narrative becomes hazy, reflecting the "world of pain" inherent in such a journey.

Acamas was a strong, deep-bodied bay horse with a white coronet on his left hind foot bred in the United Kingdom by Marcel Boussac. He was one of many successful racehorses sired by Mill Reef, an American-bred horse who won the Epsom Derby, King George VI and Queen Elizabeth Stakes and Prix de l'Arc de Triomphe in 1971. His dam Licata, was a very good racemare who won the Prix Cléopâtre and Prix de Malleret in 1972. She was even better as a broodmare, going on to produce the Grand Prix de Saint-Cloud winner Akarad and the 1982 Prix de l'Arc de Triomphe winner Akiyda. Boussac sent his colt into training with Guy Bonnaventure.

The Isiolo resort city, one of three proposed resort cities, is to be established under a public-private partnership at a cost of KSh 18.9 billion. In 2012, the Isiolo County Council was asked to set aside 2,600 ha (6,500 acres) of land at Kipsing Gap, located 20 km (12 mi) west of Isiolo town, for the project.

The building was developed from 1189 by the Knights Templar. The house (excluding its narrow rear extension) was mostly rebuilt in late 16th century, altered around 1830 and extended around the year 1928. The main house is a Grade II listed building

Before the arrival of the Spanish, the area belonged to the chieftainship of Cupules. After the conquest the area became part of the encomienda system. What is now Cuncunul was a part of three different encomiendas. The first one, which covered 1/3 of the current municipality was established as an encomienda in 1549 for Francisco Manrique, Juan de Triana, and Juan de Cárdenas. In 1607 it passed to Francisco Mallén and Francisco Mallén de Rueda and in 1645 passed to Gaspar de Ayala Pacheco and Francisca Dorantes y Solís. In 1686 Pedro Pardo de Lagos was the encomendero.

On July 2, 2013, the Baltimore Orioles acquired Scott Feldman and Steve Clevenger from the Chicago Cubs in exchange for Jake Arrieta, Pedro Strop, and international spending pool money. In Baltimore, Feldman reunited with Buck Showalter, his first big league manager. Feldman made his first start for the Orioles on July 7, 2013, against the Chicago White Sox. He held the White Sox to five hits, all singles, in a 4-1 victory. On August 18, 2013, Feldman had his best start as an Oriole, pitching a complete game shutout against the White Sox, allowing only three hits. This was the first complete game shutout of his career.

Some Catholic saints and theologians have had sometimes conflicting ideas about purgatory beyond those adopted by the Catholic Church, reflecting or contributing to the popular image, which includes the notions of purification by actual fire, in a determined place and for a precise length of time. Paul J. Griffiths notes: "Recent Catholic thought on purgatory typically preserves the essentials of the basic doctrine while also offering second-hand speculative interpretations of these elements". Thus Joseph Ratzinger wrote: "Purgatory is not, as Tertullian thought, some kind of supra-worldly concentration camp where man is forced to undergo punishment in a more or less arbitrary fashion. Rather it is the inwardly necessary process of transformation in which a person becomes capable of Christ, capable of God, and thus capable of unity with the whole communion of saints".

Willie Wood went undrafted in 1960 after playing quarterback and defensive back at Southern Cal. He wrote letters to NFL teams requesting a tryout and received a response from Vince Lombardi. The Green Bay Packers signed him as a rookie free agent on January 4, 1960. Wood became a starting safety and retired on January 19, 1972.

The GPU Technology Conference (GTC) is a global artificial intelligence (AI) conference series hosted by NVIDIA. It originated in 2009 in San Jose, California, initially focusing on solving computing challenges through GPUs. In recent years, the conference has shifted its focus to applications of AI and deep learning, including self-driving cars, healthcare, and high-performance computing. GTC 2018 attracted over 8,400 attendees. Due to the coronavirus pandemic, GTC 2020 was shifted to an online event, attracting over 45,000 registered attendees.

If a state or local government pays individuals for services performed to be relieved from unemployment, the payments to the individuals are exempt from FICA tax. The services must not be performed by individuals under other types of programs. Payments are not exempt from FICA tax if the program's primary purpose is to increase an individual's chances of employment by providing training and work experience.

The play's transitions between past and present reveal the enduring impact of historical trauma on contemporary Native American life. Depictions of shared ancestry and lineage highlight the dynamics between ancestral experiences and the challenges faced by current descendants. This historical context is crucial for understanding the ongoing marginalization of Native Americans, particularly within reservations, where federal support is often lacking. Statistics show that Native Americans experience disproportionately high rates of mood disorders and PTSD. The life expectancy of Native Americans is also lower than the general U.S. population, and they are more likely to live in poverty.

Jack Sanborn signed with his hometown team, the Chicago Bears, as an undrafted free agent in 2022 and made the team's 53-man roster. He earned his first career start against the Miami Dolphins. In his second start against the Detroit Lions, he recorded his first two NFL sacks. Critical reception of Bob Dylan's "Sugar Baby" has been largely positive. Jacob Nierenberg included the song on a list of Dylan's 20 best songs of the 2000s. Nierenberg noted the song's American folk music influences, describing it as both familiar and novel. He highlighted the line "You went years without me, Might as well keep going now" from the chorus, suggesting a theme of lost connection. Nierenberg interprets Dylan as singing the song as much for himself as for the object of his affections.

Marjorie Franklin was interested in the relationship between a patient's environment and mental illness. To address this, she developed planned environmental therapy. This approach focused on strengthening the healthy aspects of a patient's personality to bypass symptoms. Planned environmental therapy is considered a forerunner of milieu therapy.

Gerhard Bast was born into a German national household. His father, Rudolf Bast, was a lawyer and a member of the NSDAP. In 1912, the family moved to Amstetten, Lower Austria. Following in his father's footsteps, Gerhard studied law at the University of Graz, where he joined the student fraternity "Germania." Like his father and uncle, Gerhard bore scars from his time in the fraternity. He graduated with a doctorate in law. In 1931, Gerhard followed his father's lead and joined the NSDAP. The following year, he joined the SS. After graduating, he worked at the county court in St. Pölten, but lost his job due to his Nazi activities. He then worked at a bank in Linz.

After its discovery in August 2010 near the village of Yukagir in the Republic of Sakha, Yuka was initially stored in the local permafrost. The mummified remains were then studied by scientists, including Albert Protopopov from the Sakha Academy of Sciences (Yakutia). The Yuka mammoth was transported to Moscow, where it has been on display since at least 2016. Yuka is regarded as one of the best-preserved mammoths ever discovered.

Tropical Storm George originated on September 27 from a strong tropical wave northeast of the Lesser Antilles and southeast of Bermuda. A large system, it moved to the northwest and later west-northwest. George began strengthening on September 30 as it decreased its forward speed. The next day, while remaining nearly stationary south of Bermuda, a nearby ship reported that George had reached hurricane status. It continued moving very slowly, passing only 100 mi (160 km) south of Bermuda. The island experienced winds of 30 to 40 mph (48 to 64 km/h). Aside from rainbands, little impact was reported on Bermuda.

In the United States, most colleges and universities award letter grades (A, B, C, D, and F) for each class. These letter grades can be modified with plus or minus signs (e.g., A+, B-). GPA, a measure of academic achievement, is typically calculated on a scale of 0 to 4, with 4.0 corresponding to an A. The average GPA is 3.3 at private and 3.0 at public institutions.

In February 2015, Rui Pinto, a Portuguese citizen, moved to Hungary permanently, stating that Portugal was only suitable for holidays. He and his father traded in ephemera from the industrial revolution and the early twentieth century, such as old posters, photographs, railway maps, and flyers, which they sold online. Pinto maintained his interest in football and, in the summer of 2015, acquired sensitive documents related to Doyen Sports Investments. Subsequently, Pinto, using the alias "Artem Lubozov" and a Russian email address, contacted Nélio Lucas, Doyen's manager. He demanded a large sum of money, between 500,000 and one million euros, in exchange for not publishing the documents. Pinto later claimed his intention was to confirm the wrongfulness of Doyen's actions based on their willingness to pay. The "Football Leaks" case, which Pinto initiated, exposed tax fraud and corruption within European football. A Portuguese criminal case confirmed some of Pinto's suspicions.

Church of the Resurrection worship services have been described by news reports as "high octane" and "spirit-filled and Bible-based but centered on ancient liturgy," featuring a "combination of charismatic preaching and dramatic liturgy with priests wearing traditional robes and reading from the Book of Common Prayer." After retirement as a football player, Oblak started coaching at various Slovenian clubs, including three times at Olimpija Ljubljana, NK Naklo and FC Koper. Polysome profiling and ribosome profiling are two popular techniques used to study translation. Ribosome profiling, which can measure genome-wide translation dynamics at sub-codon resolution, often employs cycloheximide (CHX) to arrest ribosomes, although CHX can introduce biases. Polysome profiling separates cell extracts, typically using sucrose density gradient (SDG) centrifugation. In this method, actively translated mRNAs associated with multiple ribosomes, known as "heavy polysomes," migrate faster than free mRNAs or mRNAs associated with a single ribosome (monosomes). The optical density of RNAs is quantified across the gradient, revealing the translational status of mRNAs, with increasing numbers of ribosomes associated with polysomes. The presence of mRNA across the gradient reflects its translation status. Polysome profiling is optimally applied to track the translational status of an identified mRNA.

David Zurawik of *The Baltimore Sun* praised the film's exploration of the Boy Scouts' discriminatory policies, noting that director Tom Shepard reveals the inconsistency of the Boy Scouts as an institution that claims to be non-political while actively engaging in political discrimination. By 1977, three episodes of the first season of *Old Friends ... New Friends* had been filmed, intended to be shown to station managers that summer. The total funding amount agreed upon by the Richard King Mellon Foundation for the first season was not enough to cover the costs of production. Agnes Gund is Founder and Chair Emerita of Studio in a School. Now in its fifth decade, Studio in a School has provided visual art instruction led by professional artists to over one million students through its New York City School Programs. Since its founding in 1977, Studio has partnered with over 800 schools and community-based organizations throughout the five boroughs of New York City. Every year, more than 100 professional artists devote some 45,000 hours to over 32,000 pre-k through high school students, often in schools that would otherwise lack visual arts instruction. About 90 percent of all children who participate in Studio programs come from low-income families. Studio's New York City School Programs include the multi-year, full-time Long Term Program, an Early Childhood Program, and more flexible Residency Programs.

The vectors for Middelburg virus (MIDV) are not well-characterized, but the virus was first isolated from *Ae. caballus* mosquitoes. Entomological surveys in South Africa between 1957 and 1970 identified *Ae. circumluteolus*, *Ae. albocephalus*, and *Ma. africana* as carriers. Currently, MIDV's presence is confirmed only in Africa. However, the possibility of undetected circulation in other countries exists. The closely related Sindbis virus (SINV) has spread outside Africa, causing mild symptoms like fever and rash in humans. Horses cannot act as vectors because they develop viremia for only a short time.

ScienceSaves is a Center for Inquiry (CFI) campaign to champion science. It launched National Science Appreciation Day on March 26th. In 2021, CFI received proclamations from ten state governors. In 2022, Virginia recognized March 26 as National Science Appreciation Day.
Minot is located in north-central North Dakota, with coordinates 48.230164, -101.291199. It is 110 miles from Bismarck. The Souris River, also called the Mouse River, flows through Minot.

Scientists employ a variety of methods to study cirrus cloud properties. Lidar instruments, including spaceborne and microlidars flown on stratospheric balloons, provide fine-scale descriptions of cloud structures, with the latter offering unprecedented sensitivity to thin cirrus. Balloon-carried hygrometers measure humidity within cirrus clouds, though their measurements may not capture the full range of ice particle sizes, particularly those smaller than 100 μm. Radar units, such as scanning radars and KAZR, offer information on cloud frequency and detection rates, with some radars capable of detecting a high percentage of cirrus clouds. NASA's Moderate Resolution Imaging Spectroradiometer (MODIS) uses reflected solar bands during the day and thermal emissive bands at night to measure cirrus cloud cover, utilizing a unique band at 1.38 micrometers highly sensitive to cirrus. Visual observations from aircraft or the ground provide additional information, while Particle Analysis by Laser Mass Spectrometry (PALMS) is used to analyze the residual seed core of ice crystals captured from cirrus clouds, providing insights into their formation.

In Tennessee, two-term incumbent Republican senator Lamar Alexander, re-elected with 60% of the vote in 2008, sought re-election. On August 7, Alexander won the Republican nomination, defeating multiple challengers, including Joe Carr.

Developing as a tropical depression on October 18 near Palau Island, Hester gradually intensified as it moved westward towards the Philippines. Across the Philippines, Hester was responsible for six deaths and ₱5 million in damage. After passing over Mindanao and the Visayas as a tropical storm between October 20 and 21, the storm intensified into a typhoon before striking Palawan. Once over the South China Sea, Hester further strengthened and ultimately attained peak winds of 165 km/h (105 mph). On October 23, the storm made landfall near Huế, South Vietnam. Once onshore, Hester rapidly weakened and dissipated on October 24 over Laos.

George Blanda played quarterback and kicker at the University of Kentucky from 1945 to 1948. Paul "Bear" Bryant arrived as the new head coach during Blanda's sophomore year, following a 1-9 season in 1945. Bryant later gained fame and set countless records at the University of Alabama. Korean personal names, including all Korean surnames and most Korean given names, are based on Hanja. Korean names are traditionally written with the family name first, followed by the given name. While the use of Hanja is declining, it is still sometimes used on business cards, often for aesthetic purposes. Older generations tend to display their names in Hanja on business cards, while the younger generation often prefers to use Hangul. A typical Korean personal name consists of three syllables, with the first being the family name and the remaining two forming the given name. While most Korean family names are one syllable, some exceptions exist, such as HWANGBO. The traditional composition of a Korean given name is two syllables.

Louisville was mentioned as a potential candidate for an expansion team in National Women's Soccer League as early as 2018, shortly after construction began on their soccer-specific venue, Lynn Family Stadium. The ownership group of USL Championship club Louisville City FC began preliminary discussions with the league the following year, with plans to enter in the 2020 season. Louisville's NWSL expansion team was officially announced on October 22, 2019, sharing its ownership group with Louisville City FC. It would begin play in 2021 at Lynn Family Stadium.

A highly effective vaccine for yellow fever was developed in the 1930s. The 17D vaccine received licensing approval in 1938. A single dose provides lifelong protection for most healthy adults and children. The vaccine provides effective immunity within 10 to 30 days for 80-100% and more than 99% of people vaccinated, respectively. Common reactions are generally mild and include headaches, muscle aches, and low-grade fevers. Adults 60 years or older should receive the vaccine only if traveling to endemic countries, as there is a very low risk of developing a severe complication. The vaccine is not usually administered to infants under 6-9 months, pregnant women, individuals with weakened immune systems, or those with severe egg allergies. Since 2006, more than 105 million people have been vaccinated in mass campaigns in West Africa.

Tri-Chandra Multiple Campus, originally named Tribhuvan Chandra Intermediate College, holds the distinction of being Nepal's first institution of higher education, established in 1918 by Chandra Shumsher Rana. A year later, the college introduced science at the Intermediate level, marking the beginning of formal science education in Nepal. In 1924, it was renamed Tri-Chandra College, and bachelor's level classes commenced. Initially, the college was affiliated with the University of Calcutta, later transitioning to Patna University. Until 1951 (2007 BS), access to the college was restricted to the elite members of the Rana regime. The primary purpose of science education at the time was to prepare students for technical studies in India. Science teaching was upgraded to the Bachelor level (B.Sc.) in 1945. In 1959, Tri-Chandra College was constituted into Tribhuvan University.

Beginning in May 2009, the Pakistan Air Force (PAF) utilized its F-16 multirole fighter jets to attack militant positions in North-West Pakistan, specifically in the Swat Valley and South Waziristan. These F-16s targeted mountain hide-outs, training centers, and ammunition depots. By July 2009, PAF F-16s had flown over 300 combat missions against militants in the Swat Valley and more than 100 missions in South Waziristan. Prior to the attacks, ten F-16s were equipped with high-resolution, infrared sensor pods supplied by the United States for detailed reconnaissance of the Swat Valley. The F-16s were also used to reconnoitre the Peochar Valley with DB-110s.

Studies in Latin America have demonstrated a clear link between inadequate water and sanitation services and the incidence of diseases, particularly affecting vulnerable populations. Low-income urban residents are more likely to suffer from gastrointestinal illnesses like diarrhea, as well as "poverty diseases" such as cholera, trachoma, and schistosomiasis. Over time, consumption of contaminated water can lead to malnutrition, skin diseases, and even cancer. These health disorders stem from water pollution and the lack of safe water for drinking and personal sanitation. Notably, Latin America experienced infectious disease epidemics in 1995, including dengue and Venezuelan equine encephalitis.

There are five bus routes that intersect the parish, all focusing on the town of Kington and operated by Kington-based Sargeants Brothers. Two are the south-east to north-west routes from Hereford to Llandrindod Wells, which run along the A44 Stanner Road. A circular route runs from Kington through the north-east of Kington Rural, to Combe Moor in Byton, then north-west to Presteigne, then west to New Radnor via Walton, and then south-east to Kington via the A44 in Kington Rural. A fourth route begins at Kington, and runs north-east through Kington Rural to Titley, then north into Wales, to Presteigne, Knighton, Knucklas and Lloyney. A fifth route is circular within Kington town, with a Kington Rural south-west arm running along Hergest Road to the business park in Lower Hergest.  The closest England rail connections are on the on the Welsh Marches line, at Leominster railway station 14 miles (23 km) to the west, and Hereford railway station 17 miles (27 km) to the south-east. At 10 miles (16 km) north in Wales is Knighton railway station, at Knighton, on the Heart of Wales line.

Stinson competed for USA Basketball at the 1991 Pan American Games, where the team finished with a 6-1 record and won the bronze medal. The USA suffered a 73-68 loss to Cuba. To reach the medal round, the USA defeated Venezuela and Argentina. They faced Cuba again, but lost. The USA secured the bronze by defeating Canada 92-61. The Local Committees for Supply and Production (CLAP), known by its Spanish acronym CLAP, were created in 2016 by the government of Nicolás Maduro, who was the President of Venezuela at the time. Created in response to widespread food shortages, the CLAP program distributes subsidized food boxes and bags to Venezuelan families. The program is politically motivated and controlled by the Maduro government. Aristóbulo Istúriz, then Vice President of Venezuela, described CLAPs as "an expression of popular power." Allegations of corruption and political blackmail arose regarding the distribution of food. The Venezuelan human rights group PROVEA described CLAPs as a form of discrimination.

Despite the ongoing war with Russia, Ukraine has opened the area to disaster tourism. Numerous cultural works have explored the political resonance and human cost of the event.

The William S. DeLano House is a modest, L-plan, Greek Revival house clad with clapboarded. The house sits on a rubblestone foundation, and is constructed using the mortise-and-tenon framing - a technique which was becoming old-fashioned even at the time of construction. The house consists of a two-story block attached to a 1-1/2 story. A recessed porch with square-column supports fronts the wing, covering the main entrance. Greek Revival-style trim includes corner pilasters, a broad architrave and frieze strips, and a heavy classical cornice with returns.

In folk medicine in Honduras, the boiled root is used for diarrhea, dysentery, and stomach ache. The slightly roasted and ground root is used to treat intestinal worms and parasites. The crushed root is mixed with water to treat the bites of snakes. In Nicaragua the boiled root is used to prevent diarrhea; minced raw rhizomes are used to treat diarrhea, sickness, stomach upset, indigestion, and worms. In El Salvador it is used for stomachache and to prevent vomiting. In Costa Rica the boiled root is used for diarrhea, and an infusion to lower fever. It is considered useful for diarrhea as well as an emmenagogue. In Mexico the latex is used to heal wounds and the inflorescences are given to teething children. In the Amazon region of Peru it is used as a tonic, against gangrene, and as an antidote for bee and wasp stings. In Argentina, the whole plant is used to treat snakebite. In Venezuela it is used as a sudorific and as a cure for dysentery.

In 1928, Nyquist stated that the equipartition law, relating temperature to average energies, determines noise amplitude. This law, associated with Dulong and Petit, was applied to harmonic oscillators. Nyquist used the concept of normal modes of electrical oscillation.

The Slievenaglasha Wedge Tomb is linked to the legend of Glas Gaibhnenn, a magical, sea-green cow associated with abundance. While some accounts claim Balor abducted her from Spain, others suggest she belonged to Goibniu the smith, or Gaiblin, a farmer of Co. Cavan. The cow's udders produced an inexhaustible supply of milk. One tale recounts how an ill-conditioned woman tricked Glas Gaibhnenn by milking her into a sieve, causing the milk to flow away and become the 'source of the seven streams' located on the mountain. Mortified, the cow either ran away or died. The herd was supposedly housed on Tory Island.

Leon Goldfarb, a widower in the state of New York, applied for survivor benefits under the Social Security Act. Leon Goldfarb's late wife had worked as a secretary for New York City public schools for nearly twenty-five years and paid all of her social security taxes until her death in 1968. Upon his wife's death, Leon Goldfarb applied for survivor benefits but was denied. The relevant statute, 42 U.S.C § 402(f)(1)(D), mandated that surviving widowers must meet the burden of proving that they had been receiving over half of their financial support from their wives. The law made no such requirement for widows, who would be provided survivor benefits regardless of their dependency on their husbands.

The Bretagne Classic, originally named Circuit de Plouay and later the Grand-Prix de Plouay, was created in 1931 by former Tour de France doctor Berty, who used his influence to attract some of the biggest names of French cycling to the inaugural edition. While Napoleon's systematic looting of Italian art is well-documented, Italy has also faced a persistent and widespread problem of antiquities looting for decades, involving networks of looters, smugglers, and dealers. The Italian Carabinieri Command for the Protection of Cultural Heritage, also known as the Art Squad, has been particularly active in combating this illicit trade, identifying and seizing stolen artifacts, and working to dismantle these criminal enterprises that often raid archaeological sites throughout Italy. The Italian government, with the help of the Art Squad, has collected substantial evidence, tracing looted fragments to illicit excavations. For years, Italian authorities have insisted on the repatriation of illegally excavated artifacts, resulting in numerous lawsuits and the return of looted art and antiquities from prominent American institutions like the Metropolitan Museum of Art and Princeton University. Even private collections, such as that of Marion True, have been involved in the repatriation of trafficked works to Italy.

The development of models considering a solid-like contribution to viscosity began in the early 2000s, with Macías-Salinas et al. initiating this approach in 2003. They employed the Eyring equation as an analogue to describe this solid-like behavior. The Eyring equation, rooted in transition state theory, models the relationship between reaction rate and temperature, offering a broader applicability than the Arrhenius equation by encompassing gas, condensed, and mixed-phase reactions. This equation generalizes Reynolds' 1886 model of dilatancy. The Eyring equation models the absolute rates in chemical reactions. In this context, the Eyring equation uses activation energy to model the transition state energy. In Couette flow, the chemical potential gradient drives the movement of matter from one sliding surface to another.

Peter Robinson was a British-Canadian author best known for his Inspector Banks series, set in the fictional town of Eastvale in Yorkshire, England. His debut novel, *Gallows View*, published in 1987 while Robinson was preparing for his doctoral dissertation, introduced Detective Chief Inspector Alan Banks and earned Robinson a nomination for the Crime Writers of Canada Arthur Ellis Award. He would go on to win the Arthur Ellis Award seven times. Robinson taught at a number of Toronto community colleges and universities and served as Writer-in-Residence at the University of Windsor from 1992 to 1993. By the time of his death, the Inspector Banks series had been translated into 19 languages. Robinson also wrote two collections of short stories, *Not Safe After Dark and Other Stories* and *Innocence*. The novel released by Peter Robinson in 1990 was *The Hanging Valley*.

Nontrinitarian views acknowledge the pre-existence of Christ but deny his full divinity as co-equal and co-eternal with God the Father. Arius, a presbyter from Alexandria, is an example of someone who likely accepted Christ's pre-existence while denying his full divinity. Arianism, the religious belief system associated with Arius, maintains that the Son was created by God. Thomas Aquinas stated that Arius maintained that the Person of the Son of God had a beginning, using the phrase "there was a time when He was not."

Arundo is a highly invasive plant in southwestern North American rivers, and its promotion as a biofuel in other regions is of great concern to environmental scientists and land managers. Arundo donax was introduced from the Mediterranean to California in the 1820s for roofing material and erosion control in drainage canals in the Los Angeles area. Through spread and subsequent plantings as an ornamental plant, and for use as reeds in woodwind instruments, it has become naturalised throughout warm coastal freshwaters of North America, and its range continues to spread.

Corbelled houses are a distinctive architectural feature of the Karoo region around Williston, Fraserburg, and Carnarvon. Built by early trekboers (migrant stock farmers) mainly in the 1800s, these structures are considered architectural and cultural treasures. The term 'corbelled' describes their domed roofs constructed from flat dolerite and sandstone without mortar. Floors were typically made of smeared earth, coloured red with fat and oxblood, and polished with a smooth stone.

Constantijn Huygens is often considered a member of the Muiderkring, a group of leading intellectuals gathered around the poet Pieter Corneliszoon Hooft. Huygens came into contact with Anna Roemers Visscher and Hooft in 1619, and began exchanging poems with them. Aside from the Muiderkring, in the early 1630s, he was also in touch with René Descartes, Rembrandt, and Jan Lievens. He became friends with John Donne and translated his poems into Dutch. The death of his wife caused him anguish, preventing him from writing poetry for months. Eventually, inspired by Petrarch, he composed the sonnet "Op de dood van Sterre" ("On the Death of Sterre"), an elegy on her death, which he added to his unfinished *Dagh-werck*. His collected Dutch poems, *Koren-bloemen*, which included "Op de dood van Sterre", were published in 1657.

The RECLAIM Act addresses issues related to the Surface Mining Control and Reclamation Act (SMCRA), enacted on August 3, 1977. The primary purpose of *The Atheist Experience* is to facilitate discussions or debates on the existence of gods and related topics between theist callers and the atheist hosts and co-hosts. Primarily geared towards a non-atheist audience, the show tends to foster confrontational debates. The conversations are intended for theists to hear directly from atheists, rather than from other theists, what atheists actually believe. Another goal of the webcast is to encourage theists to question their theistic beliefs.

Due to the predominantly practising Christian population at the turn of the 19th century, many churches were built in Caerau. However, with a sharp decline in practising Christians, many of these buildings are now derelict or demolished. St. Cynfelin church is still open and regularly attended.

Kunitarō Sawamura came from a theatrical family, as did his wife Tomoko Makino. His younger brother was actor Daisuke Kato, and his older sister was actress Sadako Sawamura. Tomoko Makino's father was Shozo Makino, a film director known as the "father of Japanese cinema." Through his sister Sadako, Sawamura's brothers-in-law were Hiroyuki Nagato and Masahiko Tsugawa. Kunitarō Sawamura and Tomoko Makino had children, including Masahiro Makino, who became a film director and married Hibari Misora, and Masayuki Makino, who founded the Okinawa Actors School.

The PCT was established as a national scenic trail by the National Trails Systems Act of 1968. The two groups merged in 1987, and in 1992, the name was changed to the Pacific Crest Trail Association. The Association hired its first paid staff in 1993.

Chilmark is served by Chilmark and Fonthill Bishop Church of England Aided Primary School, a voluntary aided school. In 1971, the school merged with Fonthill Bishop, after which the Fonthill Bishop school building closed.

The Murraylands region offers abundant recreational opportunities, including boating, kayaking, water skiing, and fishing along the River Murray. Many sport facilities, reserves, parks, and trails cater to walking, cycling, and horse riding. A major government program has funded the Murray Coorong Trail initiative, which will eventually connect loop trails and experiences for 250 kilometres (160 miles) from Cadell in the Riverland to Salt Creek in the Coorong National Park. As of 2021, a 5-kilometre (3.1 miles) walking trail had been completed at Parnka Point in Coorong National Park.

Edmund Stonelake's public life began around 1892. By 1895, he identified as a Socialist and became involved in local organisations. In 1904, he was elected to the Aberdare Urban District Council, becoming chairman in 1909-10. Before World War I, Stonelake advocated for improved housing conditions. He lost his seat on the council in 1922 following a corruption scandal.

Launched on 2 December 1917, she was completed on 26 January 1918 and commissioned the same day. She was assigned the pennant number G23 in January 1918; it was changed to G09 in April 1918, and to D26 during the interwar period.

Emperor Menelik's health deterioration since 1906 led to increasing rivalries in the government. Empress Taytu asserted her authority by undermining the crown council of ministers. The Shewan faction running the daily governance was frustrated by the decisions of Empress Taytu, after she maneuvered her own candidates into positions of power. Welde Giyorgis with an army of thirty thousand men under his command did not commit himself to the Empress or the Shewan establishment, rather he ignored to obey orders or to answer letters from the central government.

Many literary works have been published about the Gulag by former prisoners. Three of the most famous such Russian writers are Aleksandr Solzhenitsyn, Eugenia Ginzburg, and Varlam Shalamov. A Polish writer Gustaw Herling-Grudziński was liberated from a camp in 1941, left Soviet Union with Polish Armed Forces in the East and published his A World Apart in 1951. A number of ex-prisoners wrote memoirs describing their experiences in the camps, many of which have been translated into English.

The St. Albans School of Public Service is a summer program for rising high school seniors established at St. Albans School. The program lasts for four weeks each summer, beginning sometime in June. Approximately 40-45 students are competitively selected from across the United States and internationally to participate in the program, which is located in Washington, D.C. Students are admitted based on academic achievement, promise, and demonstrated interest in government, public policy, and/or public service. Through classroom experiences, field trips, and a speakers program, students undertake an in-depth exploration of government, politics, and public service. The School of Public Service is considered a decently prestigious program, and offers need-based financial aid.

Several strategies can prevent peptic ulcers, particularly for individuals requiring NSAIDs. For those at low cardiovascular risk, cotherapy with a proton pump inhibitor (PPI) or misoprostol alongside NSAIDs is recommended. COX-2 inhibitors like celecoxib may reduce ulcer incidence compared to non-selective NSAIDs. PPIs are commonly used for prophylaxis and treatment of NSAID-related injuries. High doses of H2-receptor antagonists like famotidine can prevent NSAID-related gastric ulcers. However, gastrointestinal side effects and frequent dosing limit misoprostol's use. For patients at high cardiovascular risk, naproxen with a PPI may be appropriate, but avoiding COX-2 inhibitors is prudent. Acetaminophen may be an alternative to NSAIDs.

Spencer Chamberlain was born in Chapel Hill, North Carolina, and raised in Greensboro, North Carolina, with his mother and older brother, Phil, who is also a musician. As a child, Chamberlain's family had the financial means for him to attend Greensboro Day School. During his elementary school years at Greensboro Day School, his parents divorced. Chamberlain later attended Page High School. Shortly after graduating high school, he moved to Florida. Chamberlain struggled with substance use disorders, particularly a serious addiction to cocaine. He credits his stepbrother, a pastor, with introducing him to Christianity, which, along with music, he claims saved him. His cousin and step-father is Increase Chamberlain Jr. His father was John, the son of Thomas, the son of Daniel, the son of William Chamberlain who immigrated from England in 1835.

After Muhammad's death, his son, Hussein Tawfiq al-Assad, took control of the criminal network and its armed contingent. He went on to rebrand and reorganize it as official paramilitary unit, and around late June/early July 2015 officially "founded" the "Lions of Hussein Brigade". According to regional expert Aymenn Jawad Al-Tamimi, the new name of the group could be understood as referring both to Hussein Tawfiq al-Assad himself as well as Imam Husayn ibn Ali. The reorganization aimed at reducing the group's previous negative image that had resulted from its association with Muhammad's criminal activities. According to 2017 reports by the Austrian Armed Forces' Truppendienst magazine and a Clingendael Institute researcher the militia became part of the Russian-backed and Republican Guard-led 4th Corps. A commander of the Lions of Hussein later denied any affiliation with the 4th Corps or Russia. The Lions of Hussein consequently fought for the government in various war zones, participating in the Palmyra offensive (July–August 2015) against the Islamic State of Iraq and the Levant (ISIL), the Al-Ghab offensive (July–August 2015), the offensive to recapture Marj al-Sultan airbase in late 2015, and the 2015–16 Latakia offensive. Meanwhile, the unit reportedly continues its "criminal activities on the side".

George Hebard Maxwell was known for promoting comprehensive irrigation projects in the American West. In his book *Our National Resources and How to Prevent Their Exploitation*, he argued for the comprehensive damming of the Colorado River to prevent its water from flowing into the Gulf of California. Maxwell's *Patriotism of Peace* was published in 1907. He claimed Japanese colonists were colonizing Baja California and referred to Southern and Eastern European immigrants as "pauper labor." He preached that Homecroft communities would Americanize these immigrants. He also promoted policies affecting land ownership by Asians in California.

Seeking refuge and treatment for his mental health, he committed himself to the Saint-Paul-de-Mausole psychiatric asylum, a former monastery nestled in the foothills of the Alpilles mountains, near Arles. Rested and reinforced, the battery handed its guns over to 73rd (SA) Siege Bty, also being rested from the Ypres fighting, and moved out with that battery's guns on 8 November, joining a long convoy of heavy artillery marching south to reinforce Third Army for the Battle of Cambrai. (To maintain secrecy the cover story was that they were heading to the Italian Front.) During the journey it collected two new spare howitzers, to ensure that it would be able to keep guns in action. It pulled its guns into camouflaged positions on the outskirts of Gouzeaucourt on 13 November, coming under 87th HAG in III Corps. The plan was to break through the Hindenburg Line with a tank attack following a massive surprise bombardment using Predicted fire. III Corps' objective was the Marcoing–Masnières line. Zero was 06.20 on 20 November, and by 10.00 the rapid advance of the tanks and infantry had driven the enemy out of range of the battery's guns.

In corporate finance, a company's capital structure refers to the mix of debt and equity it uses to finance its operations. While strategic use of debt can be an essential tool for business growth and potentially increase shareholder returns, excessive debt can increase the likelihood of default and limit a company's flexibility to invest in value-creating opportunities. These trade-offs become more complex for companies with international operations. Raj Aggarwal's research has demonstrated that the average levels of debt used by companies vary significantly across different regions. These variations are influenced by factors such as national preferences for bank- versus market-oriented financing systems. Aggarwal's work has also examined the financing activities of the 300 largest banks in the world, showing that the size of a bank's home economy and the degree of internationalization are the two primary determinants of financing activities for the largest banks.

In late 2004, in response to Interpride's decision that the Israeli group Jerusalem Open House would host the second World Pride parade in 2005, QUIT! launched a Boycott World Pride campaign. The boycott picked up steam after the event was postponed until August 2006 because of the pullout of the Israeli settlements from the Gaza Strip. QUIT and many other individuals and organizations contended that it was inappropriate to hold an event titled "Love Without Borders" in a city bisected by a 30-foot concrete wall. The International Gay and Lesbian Human Rights Commission (IGLHRC) decided, in March 2006, not to participate in World Pride Jerusalem, stating, "IGLHRC recognizes that many LGBTI people in the region who wish to attend World Pride 2006, named 'Love without Borders,' will be unable to do so due to travel restrictions and conditions that limit mobility and participation."

Lambert signed his first professional cycling contract with Falcon cycling team in 1972 under Albert Hitchen the Falcon team manager. His first win as a pro was on Stage Two of the Tour of the West, into Penzance in Cornwall. He rode the 1972 UCI Road World Championships which took place on the 6 August 1972 in Gap, France but punctured twice and did not finish. Lambert signed with the Holdsworth cycling team for 1974 alongside Les West and Colin Lewis and won the British National Road Race Championships for the first time that year. He raced and finished Paris-Roubaix in 1977 before signing for Viking in 1978. At the 1979 World Championship at Valkenburg aan de Geul Lambert finished in 26th place of just 44 riders finishing including no other British finishers. In 1980 Lambert signed with Weinmann and finished third in the Druivenkoers Overijse behind Fons De Wolf and Rudy Pevenage and Lambert pipped Bill Nickson to the 1980 National Road Championship by an inch for his second British champion title in Redditch. Lambert joined Falcon in 1981 and then spent six seasons there. He rode the 1982 World Championship at Goodwood and in 1983 Lambert won the National Criterium Championships. In total Lambert won 64 professional races between 1972 and 1987.

In January 2015, Netflix COO Ted Sarandos stated that Jessica Jones the series was "eligible to go into multiple seasons for sure" and Netflix would look at "how well [they] are addressing both the Marvel fanbase but also the broader fanbase" in terms of determining if additional seasons would be appropriate. In July 2015, Sarandos said some of the Defender series would "selectively have multiple seasons as they come out of the gate," with series showrunner Melissa Rosenberg saying she was hopeful Jessica Jones would get an additional season before The Defenders. Rosenberg later expanded on this, saying that Marvel Television and Netflix were working out the placement of a potential second season, though "[i]t might not be possible from a logistical standpoint" to have a second season of Jessica Jones debut before The Defenders; Sarandos later confirmed this to be the case, stating that the season would air after The Defenders released in 2017. On January 17, 2016, Netflix ordered a second season of 13 episodes. Raelle Tucker joined the season as an executive producer and writer, replacing Liz Friedman from the first season, who departed the series to work on the pilot for the ABC series, Conviction.

Justice Stephen Breyer wrote an opinion that was joined by Justices O'Connor and Souter. Based on precedent set by Buckley v. Valeo, Breyer believed that some restrictions on expenditures by political parties were constitutional. Even so, he found that the Party Expenditure Provision of the FECA did not apply to an independent expenditure made by a political party without coordination with a candidate. This meant that the independent advertisement paid for by the Colorado Republican Party was not in violation of the Party Expenditure Provision, and was protected by the First Amendment.

As of 2018, Princesa Tower is planned to reach a height of 680 meters (2,230 ft). Among its features is a 550-meter (1,800 ft) "sky ride" with a carrying capacity of 76 people. A water curtain is planned, with waters falling from a height of 176 meters (577 ft), intended to be the longest man-made curtain in the world. The tower is also planned to host a restaurant, a leisure area suitable for activities such as zumba, and a glass floor area.

Washing Machine received generally positive reviews from music critics. Stephen Thomas Erlewine of AllMusic opined that the album is "easily [the band's] most adventurous, challenging and best record since Daydream Nation ... Not only are the songs more immediate than most of the material on their earlier records, the sound here is warm and open, making Washing Machine their most mature and welcoming record to date ... Washing Machine encompasses everything that made Sonic Youth innovators, and shows that they can continue to grow, finding new paths inside their signature sound". As of the 2000 census, there were 4,877 people residing in Mission Bay, Florida, comprising 1,953 households and 1,511 families. The population density was 94.5 people per square kilometer.
